title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a photographer called Maya talking about a club project for young photographers. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a photographer called Maya talking about a club project for young photographers. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A photographer called Maya introduces a photography club project.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Maya, and I run the Riverton Photography Club for young people aged fourteen to eighteen. The club began because several students were taking pictures individually but had nowhere to display them. At first, we met in the school art room. Once members from other schools began joining, we needed a larger public space. We now hold our Saturday sessions upstairs in the old [9]market hall[/9], not in the museum as some people assume. It is bright, central and has plenty of interesting details for photography.||Each term has a theme. Last term was about people at work, and members photographed shopkeepers and gardeners after asking permission. This term’s theme is [10]architecture[/10], so we will focus on bridges, roofs, doorways and unusual new buildings. Members often believe that an expensive camera is necessary. It is wonderful to have one, but it is not essential: beginners may use a [11]phone camera[/11] for all the club activities, provided the pictures are clear enough to discuss. Our tutors concentrate on composition and observation rather than equipment.||During the first outdoor session, everyone will visit the riverside. Instead of simply photographing boats and trees, members will experiment with [12]reflections[/12] in the water and in the glass windows of nearby cafés. Please bring a waterproof jacket in case it rains; we will not cancel unless the weather is very bad. Photographs taken during the project will not be printed immediately. We are creating an [13]online gallery[/13] so families and friends can view selected images and leave positive comments. Members choose which of their pictures they would like included.||We also organise specialist sessions. A landscape photographer visited us last year, and many students enjoyed hearing about mountain trips. This term, a local newspaper photographer is leading a [14]portrait workshop[/14], showing how to help people feel relaxed in front of a camera. As photographs may include members of the public, anyone under eighteen must give us a signed [15]permission form[/15] from a parent or carer before attending outdoor sessions. This is needed even if you have taken photographs independently before. At the end of the project, judges will select one image from each age group. The winner will receive a [16]book voucher[/16] rather than camera equipment, because it offers a wider choice of prize.||Several students have asked what happens next. During the summer holidays, returning members can try [17]night photography[/17], using lights around the river safely with adult supervision. It is one of our most popular activities, but not suitable for first meetings. Finally, our best images will help the local community too. We considered selling postcards, but printing small numbers was expensive. Instead, we will produce a [18]charity calendar[/18], with sales supporting the town’s youth arts centre. Registration opens tomorrow; I have forms here for anybody who is interested.

dialogue.speaker: maya
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Maya, and I run the Riverton Photography Club for young people aged fourteen to eighteen. The club began because several students were taking pictures individually but had nowhere to display them. At first, we met in the school art room. Once members from other schools began joining, we needed a larger public space. We now hold our Saturday sessions upstairs in the old market hall, not in the museum as some people assume. It is bright, central and has plenty of interesting details for photography.||Each term has a theme. Last term was about people at work, and members photographed shopkeepers and gardeners after asking permission. This term’s theme is architecture, so we will focus on bridges, roofs, doorways and unusual new buildings. Members often believe that an expensive camera is necessary. It is wonderful to have one, but it is not essential: beginners may use a phone camera for all the club activities, provided the pictures are clear enough to discuss. Our tutors concentrate on composition and observation rather than equipment.||During the first outdoor session, everyone will visit the riverside. Instead of simply photographing boats and trees, members will experiment with reflections in the water and in the glass windows of nearby cafés. Please bring a waterproof jacket in case it rains; we will not cancel unless the weather is very bad. Photographs taken during the project will not be printed immediately. We are creating an online gallery so families and friends can view selected images and leave positive comments. Members choose which of their pictures they would like included.||We also organise specialist sessions. A landscape photographer visited us last year, and many students enjoyed hearing about mountain trips. This term, a local newspaper photographer is leading a portrait workshop, showing how to help people feel relaxed in front of a camera. As photographs may include members of the public, anyone under eighteen must give us a signed permission form from a parent or carer before attending outdoor sessions. This is needed even if you have taken photographs independently before. At the end of the project, judges will select one image from each age group. The winner will receive a book voucher rather than camera equipment, because it offers a wider choice of prize.||Several students have asked what happens next. During the summer holidays, returning members can try night photography, using lights around the river safely with adult supervision. We considered selling postcards, but printing small numbers was expensive. Instead, we will produce a charity calendar, with sales supporting the town’s youth arts centre. Registration opens tomorrow; I have forms here for anybody who is interested.
